Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

We have seen this in several cases:

- https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/11530
- https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/12698
- https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/11885#issuecomment-1308346028

Today I stumbled upon this bug myself and found out why. If the user is installing to a location that is not user writable, `conda init` might ask for `sudo` access. Then it hits the `conda.gateways.subprocess` bug, where `stdin` has been passed as `str` from `conda.core.initialize` but it expected `bytes` instead.

According to the [`subprocess.Popen.communicate()` docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.communicate):

> Interact with process: Send data to stdin. Read data from stdout and stderr, until end-of-file is reached. Wait for process to terminate and set the [returncode](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.returncode) attribute. The optional input argument should be data to be sent to the child process, or None, if no data should be sent to the child. **If streams were opened in text mode, input must be a string. Otherwise, it must be bytes.**

(emphasis mine)

I think `conda.gateways.subprocess` needs to do some checks to ensure type consistency across streams.

This builds towards the goal of being able to cross-build NumPy without requiring code execution on the target environment, or an emulator thereof.

With this patch, setting the `longdouble_format` key on `properties` section of the Meson cross file, will skip the runtime check.

Example:
```
...

[properties]
longdouble_format = 'IEEE_DOUBLE_LE'
```

It may be possible to rewrite the check to entirely skip the need of running code on the target at build time, but I haven't looked into that at this point. That would be a possible future Any guidance on the best course of action as of now? Thanks in advance :smiley:

Sorry for the delay! I wasn't able to look at this earlier, but I am finally catching up with my notifications.

For Python 3.12 and forward, Debian seems to now be patching `sysconfig`. The main thing to keep in mind is that thair patching is also present inside virtual environments, so you need to make sure you only use `platlib` from `deb_system` when you aren't in a virtual environment, or similar. This can be achieved by looking at `sysconfig.get_default_scheme()`, which will return `posix_local` when you are on the global environment.

So, you'd change	

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/f05674ac43c7dd2d82f37b7c702ff71366bffefb/dev.py#L335-L348

to

```python
def get_site_packages(self):
    """
    Depending on whether we have debian python or not,
    return dist_packages path or site_packages path.
    """
    if sys.version_info < (3, 12) and 'deb_system' in INSTALL_SCHEMES:
        # debian patched python in use
        install_cmd = dist.Distribution().get_command_obj('install')
        install_cmd.select_scheme('deb_system')
        install_cmd.finalize_options()
        plat_path = Path(install_cmd.install_platlib)
    elif sysconfig.get_default_scheme() == 'posix_prefix':
        # debian patched python in use, and we are on the global environment
        # posix_prefix is the user-installable debian scheme
        plat_path = Path(get_path('platlib', scheme='deb_system'))
    else:
        plat_path = Path(get_path('platlib'))
    return self.installed / plat_path.relative_to(sys.exec_prefix)
```

However, I'd actually respect Debian's patching and install to `posix_local` unless you have a reason not to. I don't know the motivation behind doing `install_cmd.select_scheme('deb_system')` in your current code, but I suspect it might have just been copied from somewhere else.

The adjusted code:

```python
def get_site_packages(self):
    """
    Depending on whether we have debian python or not,
    return dist_packages path or site_packages path.
    """
    if sys.version_info < (3, 12) and 'deb_system' in INSTALL_SCHEMES:
        # debian patched python in use
        install_cmd = dist.Distribution().get_command_obj('install')
        install_cmd.finalize_options()
        plat_path = Path(install_cmd.install_platlib)
    else:
        plat_path = Path(get_path('platlib'))
    return self.installed / plat_path.relative_to(sys.exec_prefix)
```

No special handling is needed for Debian anymore because they now patch `sysconfig`.

That is, unless you actually want to install to the Debian install directory (`/usr/lib/python3.12/dist-packages`), which is usually reserved for Debian packages, instead of the Debian-adjusted user install directory (`/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages`).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1609637853/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

There are several use cases that are desirable to be customizable that could be tackled by implementing a "repo interaction" plugin. Some examples are, authentication, replacing/modifying the transport layer, or even interfacing with custom repo implementations.

The idea behind this is that conda would provide a plugin that allows to completely replace the code where it interacts with the remote repository.

This has some challenges, as the current codebase is not in the best shape to implement this. And there are several parts of the code that interact with the repo and need hooking into.

Additionally, ideally, it would be good to also provide the original implementation as a plugin, so that people can re-use parts of it if they just need minor modifications.

My proposal would be when a user adds a channel, they can pass an argument specifying the "type" of channel it is (eg. `conda-simple`, `special-flavor`, etc.).

```
$ conda config --add channels https://my-project.com/conda --type special-flavor
```

Each "fetch" plugin would register all the channel types they support in the metadata. This would enable Conda to find the plugin required to handle the channel, and would even allow alternative implementations for each type to be provided (eg. a plugin that uses [powerloader](https://github.com/mamba-org/powerloader) as an alternative to the default Conda "fetch" handler), promoting innovation and giving power back to the community.

```
$ conda config --add channels https://my-project.com/conda --type special-flavor
This channel requires a Conda plugin. There are 2 plugins that implement support for this channel type, please select one to install:
  1) special-plugin
     Description: Plugin to interact with Special(TM) channels
     Author: Mega Corporation
  2) special-plugin-multithreaded
     Description: Multithreaded plugin to interact with Special(TM) channels
     Author: Your Friendly Neighbourhood OSS Maintainer
```

---

This is just a proposal, but while considering alternatives or tweaks to this scheme, it is **critical** to not add any dependencies on the transport layer, as the whole point of this plugin is to abstract that away.

---

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

There are several use cases that are desirable to be customizable that could be tackled by implementing a "repo interaction" plugin. Some examples are, authentication, replacing/modifying the transport layer, or even interfacing with custom repo implementations.

The idea behind this is that conda would provide a plugin that allows to completely replace the code where it interacts with the remote repository.

This has some challenges, as the current codebase is not in the best shape to implement this. And there are several parts of the code that interact with the repo and need hooking into.

Additionally, ideally, it would be good to also provide the original implementation as a plugin, so that people can re-use parts of it if they just need minor modifications.

- [ ] https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/11696

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

There are several use cases that are desirable to be customizable that could be tackled by implementing a "repo interaction" plugin. Some examples are, authentication, replacing/modifying the transport layer, or even interfacing with custom repo implementations.

The idea behind this is that conda would provide a plugin that allows to completely replace the code where it interacts with the remote repository.

This has some challenges, as the current codebase is not in the best shape to implement this. And there are several parts of the code that interact with the repo and need hooking into.

Additionally, ideally, it would be good to also provide the original implementation as a plugin, so that people can re-use parts of it if they just need minor modifications.

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

There are several use cases that are desirable to be customizable that could be tackled by implementing a "repo interaction" plugin. Some examples are, authentication, replacing/modifying the transport layer, or even interfacing with custom repo implementations.

The idea behind this is that conda would provide a plugin that allows to completely replace the code where it interacts with the remote repository.

This has some challenges, as the current codebase is not in the best shape to implement this. And there are several parts of the code that interact with the repo and need hooking into.

Additionally, ideally, it would be good to also provide the original implementation as a plugin, so that people can re-use parts of it if they just need minor modifications.

Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

`conda` does not have bullet-proof support for spaces in its environment paths. As a matter of fact, environment names with spaces are forbidden:

```
$ conda create -n "name with spaces" python

CondaValueError: Invalid environment name: 'name with spaces'
  Characters not allowed: ('/', ' ', ':', '#')
```

However, little can conda do when the base prefix already has spaces; e.g. Miniconda was installed to a place like `/Users/Jaime RGP/anaconda`. This is prevented at the `constructor` level, which forbids the user from choosing such a location. This is unfortunate because in some cases the user might have no other choice (e.g. managed installs where the only writable location is the user directory, which is created with `Name Last name` syntax).

Additionally, a user can still end up in that situation with a command like:

```
$ conda create -p "/tmp/env with spaces" python
```

This is not prevented, but a warning is emitted:

```
WARNING: A space was detected in your requested environment path:
'/tmp/env with spaces'
Spaces in paths can sometimes be problematic. 
```

This PR tries to fix some common issues with spaces, entry points and Unix shebang strings:

#### Python entry points
- Python entry points were naively using the path to `python` as is in the shebang; since a space was included in the path, the kernel parses it wrong and fails with a "file not found error". 
- Why? Because `#!/prefix/with spaces/python` is understood as run `/prefix/with` using argument `spaces/python`. 
- The fix for this borrows inspiration from `pypa/distlib` using a cool shell/python hybrid string :) Comments in the code.
- On Windows, `noarch` entry points reuse `conda`'s own launcher (`Scripts/conda.exe`). The one bundled so far had a bug with Unicode paths, but the one bundled in `conda-build` did not. This PR syncs both so the ones in conda-build, which are more recent anyway, are used.

#### Replaced hardcoded prefixes in shebangs

- Prefix replacement didn't check for the target prefix and spaces. Even a correctly built package with no spaces might acquire spaces in this step if it gets installed to a location with spaces. 
- What we do here is to escape the spaces as `\ ` (this does nothing yet) and then check for that substring in the executable path. 
- In that case, we apply the `/usr/bin/env` trick that was already being used to fix "path too long" errors. Note that this trick has a drawback: the environment MUST be active in order to use the correct executable. An alternative solution would be ideal here, but we can't assume the script will be written in Python, so the distlib trick doesn't work.

#### Info messages

I also amended some warning/info strings:

- If the prefix uses spaces, provide more information about what could go wrong.
- Quote the name or path to the freshly created environment if it contains spaces in the stdout instructions.


#### More info

If this gets through, we can accept https://github.com/conda/constructor/pull/449

	- Abstracts `requests.Session`, which handles remote interaction (HTTP(S), FTP, etc.)
- `conda.subdir_data.fetch_repodata_remote_request`
	- Fetches the repo data from the remote repo
		- Receives a URL and the target repodata file and performs an HTTP request
- `conda.core.subdir_data.SubdirData._load`
	- Loads the
- `conda.trust.signature_verification._SignatureVerification._fetch_channel_signing_data`
	- Fetches signing data from the repo

Currently, the repo interaction is implemented by a couple functions which receive the URL and repodata name. They then use `CondaSession` to access the remote, and also implement cache. Changing this is tricky as the code is closely coupled. The channel loading by `SubdirData.query_all`, but I am not sure if there are hidden places that go around that code path.

After carefully reviewing our options, I think the best design for now is replacing the url that gets passed around with a callable that fetches the data for a given repodata name. However, this is not optimal, and in my opinion it'd be good to uncouple the repodata from these steps, passing just a callable that already has a repodata in mind, however that is not possible right now.

Regarding cache, I think optimally we should have a default cache implementation, but maybe eventually allow plugin authors to re-implement it for their own repos. But there are still some other questions up in the air, like the signing data, which are currently implemented with assumptions regarding the repo transport that will need to be handled.

Even though we have a good plan for how to integrate this plugin with the conda internals, I am still not sure exactly how the plugin declaration design should be. The easiest way would be for a plugin to inject new channels directly into conda, but I am not sure if that's the UX we want, and if it isn't, it is still a question to me of how channels would be declared to specify a specific plugin/scheme to be used.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/1231033081/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

### What is the idea?

There are several use cases that are desirable to be customizable that could be tackled by implementing a "repo interaction" plugin. Some examples are, authentication, replacing/modifying the transport layer, or even interfacing with custom repo implementations.

The idea behind this is that conda would provide a plugin that allows to completely replace the code where it interacts with the remote repository.

This has some challenges, as the current codebase is not in the best shape to implement this. And there are several parts of the code that interact with the repo and need hooking into.

Additionally, ideally, it would be good to also provide the original implementation as a plugin, so that people can re-use parts of it if they just need minor modifications.

- [ ] https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/11696

SciPy 1.7.x and 1.8.x do it like this already, and both `setuptools` and `pip` install upper bounds just fine.

I had a look and this field is a pep 440 version specifier, which allows multiple comma separated clauses, including upper bounds. The issue seems to be a bug in `pep621` or `packaging` that does not allow a list.

I am not sure if we are talking about the same thing here. (comment)](https://github.com/ev-br/mc_lib/issues/45#issuecomment-939519868), I'd like to propose a different/better approach (**the only problem is that it has to depend on `distutils`**, which I believe is fine because meson [depends](https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/4267#issuecomment-921383139) on that anyway; when debian folks finally start patching `sysconfig` instead of `distutils` will be removed in 3.12 and all of this will change, so depending on it isn't really a problem.

switches to Meson in `pyproject.toml` (see gh-13615), by using the `meson-python` build backend. There's an important conceptual change: _`pyproject.toml` file is now by default usable for development and does not deal with pinning numpy specifically for PyPI - that is now done in a separate script._

This is an update/alternative to gh-15476, which wasn't going to quite work as planned (as in [this comment](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/15476#issuecomment-1111542685)). Given how the build hooks in `pyproject.toml` work, it is fundamentally not possible to have both the development/CI/etc. config in there _and_ the PyPI-specific pins. Having a separate script for generating our PyPI artifacts isn't a problem, because this should only be used for releases in well-defined CI configs. One goal I have with this new PyPI-specific script though is to make it much easier to build such wheels locally for testing; right now that's basically impossible, given the many hoops that https://github.com/MacPython/scipy-wheels jumps through. 

Still TODO:
- [x] update release procedure docs
- [x] ~implement PyPI-specific wheel build changes~ EDIT: no longer needed because `meson-python` now picks up uncommitted changes. So we should be good with what `multibuild` does for the `distutils`-based builds.
- [x] add 1.9.0 release notes
- [x] ~add user/packager-facing docs on using `pip`, `build`, etc.~ no change here in behaviour, better guidance to users about build isolation would be nice, but is decoupled from this PR.
- [x] ~implement the change to ensure wheels have the correct runtime dependency on `numpy` (>= build-time version): [gh-14541#issuecomment-968317738](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14541#issuecomment-968317738).~ EDIT: doing this in a follow-up, since the `meson-python` feature won't be available in time. That's not an issue, because this behavior was never available with our `numpy.distutils` based builds.

There is still work to do, but the sdist generation works and I'd like some feedback now on the approach.

Cc @FFY00, @matthew-brett, @tylerjereddy, @eli-schwartz, @mattip Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched for other feature requests and couldn't find a duplicate (including also the ``type-feature`` tag)
- [X] I confirmed that it's not related to another project (see the above section)

### What is the idea?

To enable improvements in the plugin system, we should decide on an approach for versioning the plugin API. There are a couple options:

1) Encode the version in the hook names

This would consist on naming the hooks something like `conda_cli_register_subcommands_v1`, etc. This is not pretty, but works and is reasonable flexible.

2) Have a Pythonic API to select the hook version

This would consist of having a dynamic way to fetch the hook version, using a decorator or function attributes. This looks better but is more of a maintenance burden

3) Create a versioned namespace

This would consist of placing the plugin hooks in a separate module or object. This solution is okay from the plugin developer POV, but having a global namespace like this is not optimal.

4) An hybrid approach

This would consist of designing the hooks in a way so that we minimize possible interface breakage, and replacing hooks by different ones, hopefully removing the necessity for hook versions. This can be done by for eg. providing custom types for returned objects, only requiring the plugins to update their implementation to use the newer types, instead of defining a different hook.
This solution is okay from the plugin developer POC, but it is unclear if it reasonably suitable for the more complicated use-cases.

---

Also worth noting: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/issues/170

I think I would prefer 4), which tries to mitigate the necessity for API versioning, but if needed, it can be done in parallel with one of the other three. At this point, I don't think we have enough information to make a call, we should get some more feedback from the implementation challenges.

(#11260)","distinct":false,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/commits/cb97f86716c95cad7cdb08af1ef4990751bb81ed"},{"sha":"c92b2062e34764e4ff0ac69b975022af98762d50","author":{"email":"5eeb9291dcba3f3719fd983e09c1a0c357f0735a@users.noreply.github.com","name":"Jaime Rodríguez-Guerra"},"message":"Add support for libmamba integrations (#11193)

- Add mamba dependencies to dev requirements (incl. Docker images) (dd847c2)
- Add `solver_logic` Enum and `_get_solver_logic()` forwarder (09c10b9)
- Tune `UnsatisfiableError` exceptions  (082ff64)
- Rework logging to allow file logs with debug level (0808ce2, e3bab36)
- Rework testing infrastructure to support downstream packages (includes fixes to original helpers for local channel serialization instead of in-memory cache override) (954e4a0, fa4d5b8, b55085b, 86dafe3, 0c72113, 0f9eb57, a255c1d)

Test changes:

- change timestamp in test data so it's one SECOND older (not just one ms) (6a5f3aa)
- Change expected logging levels (part of 0808ce2)

Other small fixes:
- Fix URL encoding / decoding (otherwise mamba fails to load local channels) (e7a4d03)
- add `fresh_context` contextmanager (needed in some downstream tests) (a025443)
- resolve: move specs conversion before logging (the logger is enabled for debug for our file logs, so this subtle bug is uncovered) (0df41a4)
- add context.override contextmanager (c8ffe7a)
- add __str__ to auxlib.Null for cleaner depiction in logs (c0c2118)
- default to REPODATA_FN is passed argument is falsey (1ad4cfb)
- fix logging typo (unimportant) (29372e5)
- temporary workaround for `.conda_build_form()` deficiencies described in #11200 (f26c643)
* Simplify deps-only tests to check if the Python version hasn't changed.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/pulls/comments/811362231","pull_request_review_id":889009454,"id":811362231,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAF8kJs4wXGe3","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,57 @@
+================
+Conda Plugin API
+================
+
+As of X.Y, Conda has support for user plugins, enabling them to extend and/or
+change some of its functionality.
+
+Plugins are implemented via pluggy_ and discovered using
+`Python package entrypoints`_. You may refeer to pluggy_'s documentation
+for a full description of its features, but in summary, you will want to define
+the hooks you want to register and register your plugin under the ``conda``
+entrypoint namespace.
+
+Example:
+
+
+.. code-block:: python
+   :caption: my_plugin.py
+
+   import conda.plugins
+
+
+   @conda.plugins.hookimpl
+   def conda_cli_register_subcommands():
+       ...
+
+
+.. code-block:: python
+   :caption: setup.py
+
+   from setuptools import setup
+
+   setup(
+       name="my-conda-plugin",
+       install_requires="conda",
+       entry_points={"eggsample": ["my_conda_plugin = my_plugin"]},","path":"docs/source/plugin-api/index.rst","position":36,"original_position":36,"commit_id":"d5e5e0cdea4216b9f3ac7cbee0de399c1a95d34b","original_commit_id":"d5e5e0cdea4216b9f3ac7cbee0de399c1a95d34b","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Yeah, but we should rename this to something more intuitive. I based this off the pluggy documentation and forgot to change this :sweat_smile: 

> Hah, yeah, eggs are the name of the file format that Python packages used to be distributed as (from the [Monty Python "SPAM" sketch](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zLih-WQwBSc)) 🤷🏻

This is why you will find `spam` in the examples all over the standard library documentation :wink: CLA"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2021-12-31T17:59:05Z","updated_at":"2022-02-28T20:32:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/pulls/11121","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This got a bit delayed with Christmas, and then I fell ill, but it is here at last.

The plugin system itself is fairly simple, it essentially consists of a few definitions in a new `conda.plugins` module.
Initially, I planned to only expose the plugin manager instance to the rest of the conda codebase via the context object, but that proved insufficient right on the first plugin implementation, as the argument parsing happens before the context object is created, and the context object creation is currently inherently dependent on the argument parsing. As such, the plugin manager is also exposed via a helper function in the context module. This might limit us in the future, but that could potentially be solved by refactoring the context object a bit.
Instead of exposing the plugin manager directly, I think we might benefit of having a wrapper around it that does all the data necessary gathering, but this is not clear to me at this point, hopefully it will become clearer as we implement more plugins and get an idea of their needs.

Overall, I think the code size is pretty small, which was my goal. It demonstrates how easily plugins can be implemented in the conda codebase.

Regarding the actual subcommand plugin implementation, I did explore improving things a little bit more in regards to the `argparse` API usage and how to plug this functionality into it, but ultimately settled on just setting `epilog` instead of overwriting `print_help`. Part of the reasoning was that jezdez expressed the wish to move away from `argparse` to a different module, like `click`, so spending more time trying to improve this design did not seem worth it. For the same reason, and backwards compatibility, I decided to give plugin users full control over the argument parsing, instead of locking them into a specific API, like integrating the hook directly into `argparse`

I still need to add the tests, but I think I am reasonably happy with the end result so far.

work","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This got a bit delayed with Christmas, and then I fell ill, but it is here at last.

The plugin system itself is fairly simple, it essentially consists of a few definitions in a new `conda.plugins` module.
Initially, I planned to only expose the plugin manager instance to the rest of the conda codebase via the context object, but that proved insufficient right on the first plugin implementation, as the argument parsing happens before the context object is created, and the context object creation is currently inherently dependent on the argument parsing. As such, the plugin manager is also exposed via a helper function in the context module. This might limit us in the future, but that could potentially be solved by refactoring the context object a bit.
Instead of exposing the plugin manager directly, I think we might benefit of having a wrapper around it that does all the data necessary gathering, but this is not clear to me at this point, hopefully it will become clearer as we implement more plugins and get an idea of their needs.

Overall, I think the code size is pretty small, which was my goal. It demonstrates how easily plugins can be implemented in the conda codebase.

Regarding the actual subcommand plugin implementation, I did explore improving things a little bit more in regards to the `argparse` API usage and how to plug this functionality into it, but ultimately settled on just setting `epilog` instead of overwriting `print_help`. Part of the reasoning was that @jezdez expressed the wish to move away from `argparse` to a different module, like `click`, so spending more time trying to improve this design did not seem worth it. For the same reason, and backwards compatibility, I decided to give plugin users full control over the argument parsing, instead of locking them into a specific API, like integrating the hook directly into `argparse`

I still need to add the tests, but I think I am reasonably happy with the end result so I don't think so, right? Even if it does, bringing new dependencies is problematic as lots of downstreams are required to unvendor those dependencies to comply to guidelines when packaging Conda. I'd highly recommend avoiding external dependencies unless absolutely necessary. A namedtuple is perfectly suitable for this use-case.
IMO this snippet should not motivate bringing any dependency. If we do decide bringing to bring in `attrs` or something like that, we could update it to use it, though even in that case I'd be inclined to not do that because this is public API and that would lock us into the dependency even if we decide to move away from it because it is no longer the best option for the code that motivated bringing it in the first place.

This would, of course, be a different story if the code did call for a functionality provided by a package like `attrs`, but in this case, it does not, and IMO that would only make the code more complex anyway :sweat_smile: work","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This got a bit delayed with Christmas, and then I fell ill, but it is here at last.

The plugin system itself is fairly simple, it essentially consists of a few definitions in a new `conda.plugins` module.
Initially, I planned to only expose the plugin manager instance to the rest of the conda codebase via the context object, but that proved insufficient right on the first plugin implementation, as the argument parsing happens before the context object is created, and the context object creation is currently inherently dependent on the argument parsing. As such, the plugin manager is also exposed via a helper function in the context module. This might limit us in the future, but that could potentially be solved by refactoring the context object a bit.
Instead of exposing the plugin manager directly, I think we might benefit of having a wrapper around it that does all the data necessary gathering, but this is not clear to me at this point, hopefully it will become clearer as we implement more plugins and get an idea of their needs.

Overall, I think the code size is pretty small, which was my goal. It demonstrates how easily plugins can be implemented in the conda codebase.

Regarding the actual subcommand plugin implementation, I did explore improving things a little bit more in regards to the `argparse` API usage and how to plug this functionality into it, but ultimately settled on just setting `epilog` instead of overwriting `print_help`. Part of the reasoning was that @jezdez expressed the wish to move away from `argparse` to a different module, like `click`, so spending more time trying to improve this design did not seem worth it. For the same reason, and backwards compatibility, I decided to give plugin users full control over the argument parsing, instead of locking them into a specific API, like integrating the hook directly into `argparse`

I still need to add the tests, but I think I am reasonably happy with the end result so work","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This got a bit delayed with Christmas, and then I fell ill, but it is here at last.

The plugin system itself is fairly simple, it essentially consists of a few definitions in a new `conda.plugins` module.
Initially, I planned to only expose the plugin manager instance to the rest of the conda codebase via the context object, but that proved insufficient right on the first plugin implementation, as the argument parsing happens before the context object is created, and the context object creation is currently inherently dependent on the argument parsing. As such, the plugin manager is also exposed via a helper function in the context module. This might limit us in the future, but that could potentially be solved by refactoring the context object a bit.
Instead of exposing the plugin manager directly, I think we might benefit of having a wrapper around it that does all the data necessary gathering, but this is not clear to me at this point, hopefully it will become clearer as we implement more plugins and get an idea of their needs.

Overall, I think the code size is pretty small, which was my goal. It demonstrates how easily plugins can be implemented in the conda codebase.

Regarding the actual subcommand plugin implementation, I did explore improving things a little bit more in regards to the `argparse` API usage and how to plug this functionality into it, but ultimately settled on just setting `epilog` instead of overwriting `print_help`. Part of the reasoning was that @jezdez expressed the wish to move away from `argparse` to a different module, like `click`, so spending more time trying to improve this design did not seem worth it. For the same reason, and backwards compatibility, I decided to give plugin users full control over the argument parsing, instead of locking them into a specific API, like integrating the hook directly into `argparse`

I still need to add the tests, but I think I am reasonably happy with the end result so CLA"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2021-12-31T17:59:05Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T13:03:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/pulls/11121","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This got a bit delayed with Christmas, and then I fell ill, but it is here at last.

The plugin system itself is fairly simple, it essentially consists of a few definitions in a new `conda.plugins` module.
Initially, I planned to only expose the plugin manager instance to the rest of the conda codebase via the context object, but that proved insufficient right on the first plugin implementation, as the argument parsing happens before the context object is created, and the context object creation is currently inherently dependent on the argument parsing. As such, the plugin manager is also exposed via a helper function in the context module. This might limit us in the future, but that could potentially be solved by refactoring the context object a bit.
Instead of exposing the plugin manager directly, I think we might benefit of having a wrapper around it that does all the data necessary gathering, but this is not clear to me at this point, hopefully it will become clearer as we implement more plugins and get an idea of their needs.

Overall, I think the code size is pretty small, which was my goal. It demonstrates how easily plugins can be implemented in the conda codebase.

Regarding the actual subcommand plugin implementation, I did explore improving things a little bit more in regards to the `argparse` API usage and how to plug this functionality into it, but ultimately settled on just setting `epilog` instead of overwriting `print_help`. Part of the reasoning was that @jezdez expressed the wish to move away from `argparse` to a different module, like `click`, so spending more time trying to improve this design did not seem worth it. For the same reason, and backwards compatibility, I decided to give plugin users full control over the argument parsing, instead of locking them into a specific API, like integrating the hook directly into `argparse`

I still need to add the tests, but I think I am reasonably happy with the end result so far.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/11121/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":2,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/11121/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/1017485148","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11121#issuecomment-1017485148","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/11121","id":1017485148,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAF8kJs48pZdc","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-20T13:03:35Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T13:03:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I have been having some issues with the tests, it seems things get cached somewhere :confused:","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/1017485148/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492/events","html_url":"https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/13492","id":1115328700,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAoMVs5CepC8","number":13492,"title":"Auto-complete hinted suggestions using the END key","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-01-26T18:02:55Z","updated_at":"2022-01-26T18:02:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Sorry if this is duplicated and has been implemented already, but I did search the issue tracker and did not find anything mentioning it.

The auto-complete hints in the new version are awesome, however I find myself trying to apply them by pressing the END key, just like I do in the fish shell, and IPython does not seem to have that keybind enabled by default.

Would it be possible considering adding this to the default keybinds? Several other auto-complete systems do the same, so I don't think I am the only user that would be benefit from this change. I think it would make the defaults slight better :blush: 

Thank you!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipython/issues/13492/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

#### Reference issue

#### What does this implement/fix?
This moves the Python build system to Meson, by using the `meson-python` backend.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/15476/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/15476/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1022610157","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/15476#issuecomment-1022610157","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/15476","id":1022610157,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc4888rt","user":{"login":"FFY00","id":11718923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzE4OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11718923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00","html_url":"https://github.com/FFY00","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FFY00/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-26T21:16:43Z","updated_at":"2022-01-26T21:16:43Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> I may still be missing something. The changes to `pyproject.toml` look good to me.

We will need `pep621` (but see https://github.com/FFY00/mesonpy/issues/33, so it's probably best to just wait on that before thinking of merging the PR).

> @FFY00 any idea why this was implemented in `pip`? It doesn't make any sense to me. We are switching build systems, which means we are going to default to Meson in `1.9.0` but will leave `setup.py` in the release as a backup.

Looks like a reasonable decision to me, but unfortunately it comes to bite us while we are in the middle of the process of migrating to PEP 517. In most situations where a PEP 517 backend is specified in `pyproject.toml`, the developers did not intend for the project to be installed without PEP 517.

Lots of users when facing issues will search for it online, and most likely some will end up finding something about PEP 517 and people telling the "asker" to disable PEP 517 as a solution. I think a big number of those users will just try out the PEP 517 disabling flag blindly, which will not work. In fact, I think it will throw them off by making it seems like it did, this is due to the way pip works, as I will explain below.

AFAIK, pip does not run `setup.py` directly, instead, it invokes setuptools itself and asks for a build. I don't know the particular reason behind that, perhaps @pradyunsg or @uranusjr do? (please don't feel any pressure to reply if you don't know this from the top of your mind, it is not very relevant to us, just something that would be nice to know)
This is one of the things that led to pip mistakenly start supporting `setup.cfg`-only projects (this means, no `pyproject.toml` and no `setup.py`), which was fixed in https://github.com/pypa/pip/pull/10031.
Other thing that contributes negatively to this situation is `setuptools` working with no metadata, it will fallback the package name to `UNKNOWN` and the version to `0.0.0`. Meaning that it will successfully build a sdist or wheel for any random directory it is run on.

```
$ mkdir test
$ cd test
$ python -c 'import setuptools; setuptools.setup()' bdist_wheel
running bdist_wheel
running build
/usr/lib/python3.10/site-packages/setuptools/command/install.py:34: SetuptoolsDeprecationWarning: setup.py install is deprecated. Use build and pip and other standards-based tools.
  warnings.warn(
installing to build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel
running install
running install_egg_info
running egg_info
creating UNKNOWN.egg-info
writing UNKNOWN.egg-info/PKG-INFO
writing dependency_links to UNKNOWN.egg-info/dependency_links.txt
writing top-level names to UNKNOWN.egg-info/top_level.txt
writing manifest file 'UNKNOWN.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
listing git files failed - pretending there aren't any
reading manifest file 'UNKNOWN.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
writing manifest file 'UNKNOWN.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
Copying UNKNOWN.egg-info to build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel/UNKNOWN-0.0.0-py3.10.egg-info
running install_scripts
creating build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel/UNKNOWN-0.0.0.dist-info/WHEEL
creating 'dist/UNKNOWN-0.0.0-py3-none-any.whl' and adding 'build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel' to it
adding 'UNKNOWN-0.0.0.dist-info/METADATA'
adding 'UNKNOWN-0.0.0.dist-info/WHEEL'
adding 'UNKNOWN-0.0.0.dist-info/top_level.txt'
adding 'UNKNOWN-0.0.0.dist-info/RECORD'
removing build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel
```

So if the user was able to disable PEP 517 in pip, I believe, as it stands currently, the project will still pass the rest of pip's validation, and pip would gladly build and install some garbage when not run on an actual valid project. This, of course, could be fixed another way, but I think pip's decision is reasonable for the reason I highlighted above.

Sorry for the dive on this, but I don't think many people know about pip's behavior here, so I thought it would be worth to share it you, even if it's just tangentially related to the actual issue :sweat_smile: ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1022610157/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
